policy_statement: NAP target: reduce the geographical distribution area of invasive alien species by 10% by 2030 compared to a reference year.
mechanism_description: The policy sets a target to reduce the geographical area covered by invasive alien species, helping protect ecosystem integrity and associated carbon storage.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem management (invasive control) → ↑ ecosystem integrity → ↑/preserved carbon stocks (biomass + soils) → ↓ net GHG emissions / ↑ carbon sinks
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Land and habitat management actions reduce the extent/abundance of invasive alien species across landscapes (e.g., forests, wetlands, riparian zones, grasslands).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Lower invasive pressure allows native community structure and ecosystem functions (productivity, nutrient cycling, fire regime, hydrology) to recover or remain stable, reducing disturbance and preventing conversion/degradation that would otherwise mobilize stored carbon from vegetation and soils.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided CO₂ emissions from biomass/soil carbon loss and, in some cases, increased net carbon sequestration due to healthier, more productive native ecosystems.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is maintaining or increasing ecosystem carbon storage by preventing invasive-species-driven degradation, thereby avoiding CO₂ emissions and/or enhancing carbon sinks.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Invasive species control improves ecosystem condition, which preserves/enhances biogenic carbon stocks—an ecosystem-mediated climate effect.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While invasive control can also reduce wildfire risk (adaptation/mitigation co-benefit) and improve land productivity (resource efficiency co-benefit), the central mechanism described is ecosystem integrity and associated carbon storage—core Nature-Based Solutions logic.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific invasive-control program is primarily justified/implemented for hazard reduction (e.g., fuel-load reduction to prevent fires) or to reduce direct emissions from management operations, the dominant pathway could shift; however, the canonical mechanism here is explicitly “ecosystem_and_carbon_preservation,” keeping NBS primary.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is invasive species control to preserve ecosystems/carbon stocks fits Nature-Based Solutions because the climate effect is mediated through ecosystem carbon storage/sink capacity.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further subtyping (e.g., forest vs wetland, adaptation co-benefits, biodiversity outcomes) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific text and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Invasive-species actions can sometimes increase near-term emissions (machinery use, herbicides) or be primarily hazard-focused (e.g., wildfire fuel management); if those become the dominant quantified objective, categorization could shift, but under this canonical mechanism the primary remains ecosystem carbon preservation (NBS).
key_indicators: “target”, “by 2030”, “reduce the geographical distribution area”, “10%”, “compared to the reference year”
co_benefits: Biodiversity protection and ecosystem integrity; potential wildfire risk reduction and improved drought resilience via healthier native ecosystems
instance_edge_case_notes: Although invasive control can be implemented via direct programs/regulation, the provided text contains only a metric/target (no mandate, funding, or operational measure), so classification is limited to target-setting rather than regulatory or investment instruments.
